flash_cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Olfactory indicators: Show odour changes in acidic or basic media. E.g. onion and clove.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Litmus solution: It is a natural indicator. It is a purple dye extracted from Lichens. Other examples are Red Cabbage and coloured petals of Petunia and turmeric.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Reaction with metal

  • Acid + Metal {tex}\to{/tex} Salt + Hydrogen
  • 2HCl + Zn {tex}\to{/tex} ZnCl2 + H2
  • 2HNO3 + Zn{tex}\to{/tex}Zn(NO3)2 + H2
  • H2SO4 + Zn {tex}\to{/tex} ZnSO4 + H2
  • 2CH3 COOH + Zn {tex}\to{/tex} (CH3COO) 2Zn + H2
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Pop test: When a burning candle is brought near a test tube containing hydrogen gas it burns with a Pop sound. This test is conducted for examining the presence of hydrogen gas.
Base + Metal {tex}\to{/tex} Salt + Hydrogen
NaOH + Zn {tex}\to{/tex} Na2ZnO + {tex}\underset{\text{ Sodium Zincate }}{{\mathrm{Na}}_2\mathrm{ZnO}}{/tex} + H2
Note– Such reactions are not possible with all the metals.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Reactions of metal oxides with acids
MetalOxide + Acid {tex}\to{/tex} Salt + Water
{tex}\mathop {CuO}\limits_{Copperoxide} + \mathop {HCl}\limits_{Hydrochloric\,\,acid} \to\mathop {CuC{l_2}}\limits_{Copper\,\,Chloride} + \mathop {{H_2}O}\limits_{Water} {/tex}
Note: Appearance of blue green colour of the solution because of formation of CuCl2.Metallic oxides are said to be basic in nature because they give salt and water on reacting with acids. Some metallic oxides react with both acids and base and are called AMPHOTERIC OXIDES.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
All acidic solutions conduct electricity because of formation of (H+)ions in aq. solution.

Glowing of bulb indicates that there is a flow of electric current through the solution.
Acids or bases in a Water Solution
HCl + H2O {tex}\to{/tex} H3O+ + Cl-
H3O+: Hydronium ion.
H+ ion cannot exist alone. It exists as H+(aq) or (H3O+) hydronium ion.
i.e. Base provide OH-(aq) ions in the presence of water
NaOH(s) {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O}}{/tex} Na + (aq) + OH-(aq)
KOH(s) {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O}}{/tex} K+(aq) + OH-(aq)
Mg(OH)2(s) {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O}}{/tex} Mg2+(aq) + 2OH-(aq)
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: The reaction of base with different solutions
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: The reaction of Acid with different solution
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Reaction of a Non-metallic Oxide with Base: Bases react with non-metallic oxides to form salt and water.
Ca(OH)2 + CO2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CaCO3 + H2O
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Reaction of Metallic Oxides with Acids: Acids react with metal to form salt and water.
Metal oxide + Acid {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Salt + Water
CuO(s) + 2HCl(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CuCl2(aq) + H2O(l)
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Metal Carbonates and Metal Hydrogen carbonates React with bases
Base + Metal Carbonate/ Metal Hydrogen Carbonate {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No Reaction
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Metal Carbonates and Metal Hydrogen carbonates React with Acids
When an acid reacts with a metal carbonate then a salt, carbon dioxide and water are produced.
Na2CO3(s) + 2HCl(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NaCl(aq) + CO2(g) + H2O(l)
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Reaction of Bases with Metals:
Base + Metal {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Salt + Hydrogen gas
2NaOH + Zn {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na2ZnO2 + H2 {tex}\uparrow{/tex}
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Reaction of Acids with Metals: When an acid reacts with a metal, then a salt and hydrogen gas is formed.
Acid + Metal {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Salt + Hydrogen gas
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Phenolphthalein is colourless in an acidic solution and gives a pink colour in a basic solution.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Methyl orange indicator gives a red colour in an acidic solution and gives a yellow colour in a basic solution.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Turmeric: Turmeric contains a yellow dye which turns red in basic solutions. A stain of curry on a white cloth becomes reddish-brown when soap is applied because soap is basic in nature
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Litmus: Litmus solution is a purple dye extracted from lichen. When the litmus solution is neither acidic nor basic, its colour is purple. An acid turns blue litmus to red and a base or an alkali turns red litmus to blue.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: OLFACTORY INDICATORS: These substances have different odour in acids and bases. E.g. Vanilla, clove oil, onion extract
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: SYNTHETIC INDICATORS: These are chemical substances. E.g. Phenolphthalein ,Methyl Orange
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: NATURAL INDICATORS: These are found in plants. E.g. Litmus, red cabbage leaves extract, flowers of hydrangea plant ,turmeric
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: INDICATORS: These are the substances which change their colour/smell in different types of solutions
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: SALTS: These are the compounds formed from reaction of acid and base Example: NaCl, KCl
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Alkalis: These are bases which are soluble in water [NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)2].
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Strong Bases: Completely ionized in water like NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)2
Weak Bases: Partially ionized in water like NH4OH
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Bases: These are the substances which are bitter in taste and soapy in touch. They turn red litmus solution blue. They give OH- ions in aqueous solution.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Concentrated Acid: Having more amount of acid + less amount of water
Dilute Acid: Having more amount of water + less amount of acid
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Strong Acids: Completely ionized in water like HCl, H2SO4, HNO3
Weak Acids: Partially ionized in water like CH3COOH, Oxalic acid, Lactic acid
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Acids: These are the substances which have sour taste. They turn blue litmus solution red.
They give H+ ions in aqueous solution. The term ‘acid’ has been derived from the Latin word, acidus, which means sour.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Salts have various uses in everyday life and in industries.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Water of crystallisation is the fixed number of water molecules chemically attached to each formula unit of a salt in its crystalline form.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Mixing concentrated acids or bases with water is a highly exothermic process.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Acidic and basic solutions in water conduct electricity because they produce hydrogen and hydroxide ions respectively.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: When an acid reacts with a metal carbonate or metal hydrogen carbonate, it gives the corresponding salt, carbon dioxide gas and water.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: When a base reacts with a metal, along with the evolution of hydrogen gas a salt is formed which has a negative ion composed of the metal and oxygen.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: When an acid reacts with a metal, hydrogen gas is evolved and a corresponding salt is formed.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Acidic nature of a substance is due to the formation of H+(aq) ions in solution. Formation of OH-(aq) ions in solution is responsible for the basic nature of a substance.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Reaction of acids and bases with each other to give salt and water are called Neutralisation Reactions
Base + Acid {tex}\to{/tex} Salt + Water
e.g. NaOH(aq) + HCl(aq) {tex}\to{/tex} NaCl(aq) + H2O(l)
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Actions of Acids with metal Carbonates and metal bicarbonates
MetalCarbonate + Acid {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Salt + Carbondioxide + Water
Na2CO3(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NaCl(aq) + CO2(g) + H2O(l)
Metalbicarnonate + Acid {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Salt + Carbondioxide + Water
NaHCO3 + HCl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} NaCl + CO2 + H2O
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Concentrated Acid: A concentrated acid contains a large amount of acid (higher concentration of hydronium ions)and a small amount of water.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Acid: Base Indicators

S.No Name of the Indicator Colour Change With Acid Colour Change with Base
A. Blue litmus solution To red No change
B. Red litmus solution No change To blue
C. Turmeric No change To red
D. Methyl orange To red To yellow
E. Phenolphthalein (colourless) No change To pink
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text: Acid: Base Indicator Substances which indicate the presence of an acid or base in a solution.
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Some naturally occurring acids

Vinegar Acetic Acid
Orange Citric Acid
Lemon Citric Acid
Tamarind Tartaric Acid
Tomato Oxalic Acid
Sour milk (Curd) Lactic Acid
Ant and Nettle sting Methanoic Ac
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Acids Bases
Sour in taste Derived from Greek word' ACIDUS' Bitter in taste
Changes blue litmus into red Changes red litmus into blue
e.g. Hydrochloric acid HCl e.g. Sodium hydroxide NaOH
Sulphuric acid {tex}{H_2}S{O_4}{/tex} Potassium hydroxide KOH
Nitric acid {tex}HN{O_3}{/tex} Calcium hydroxide {tex}{C_a}{(OH)_2}{/tex}
Acetic acid {tex}HN{O_3}{/tex} Ammonium hydroxide
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

cards.text:
Acids and Bases React with each other
The reaction between an acid and a base to form salt and water is called a neutralisation reaction
Base + Acid {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Salt + Water
NaOH(aq) + HCl(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} NaCl(aq) + H2O(l)
cards.topic_name: Understanding the Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases

flash_cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text:
Alkalis
All bases do not dissolve in water. An alkali is a base that dissolves in water. Common alkalis are:

  1. NaOH Sodium hydroxide
  2. KOH Potassium hydroxide
  3. Calcium hydroxide
  4. Ammonium hydroxide

Note: All alkalis are bases but all bases are not alkalis.
Precaution must be taken while mixing acid or base with water. The acid must always be added to water with constant stirring as it is a highly exothermic reaction.
When an acid or a base is mixed with water they become dilute. This results in the decrease in the concentration of per unit volume in acids and bases respectively, i.e. no. of H+ ion and OH- ion reduces.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text:
Preparation of NaCl:
Sodium chloride is obtained by mining the deposits and brine solution is obtained by passing water into the deposits. Hence the salts get dissolved then the solution is pumped out. Evaporation of the sea water is one of the major processes used to obtain salt. The crystals obtained usually consists of impurities such as calcium sulphate, sodium sulphate etc. Pure crystals are obtained by dissolving the salts with little water and filtering the solution.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text: A base is a substance which dissolves in water to produce hydroxide ions [OH-ions]. Bases which are soluble in water are called alkalis.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text: An acid is a substance which dissociates (or ionises) when dissolved in water to release hydrogen ions.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text: Acids and bases neutralise each other to form corresponding salts and water.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text: The strength of an acid or an alkali can be tested by using a scale called the pH scale (0-14) which gives the measure of hydrogen ion concentration in a solution.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text:
Reaction of Non Metallic Oxide with Base
Non-metallic oxide + Base {tex}\to{/tex} Salt + Water
CO2 + Ca(OH)2 {tex}\to{/tex} CaCO3 + H2O
Note: Non Metallic oxides are said to be acidic in nature because on reacting with a base they produce salt and water.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text:
CO2 the following reaction takes place
{tex}CaC{O_{3(s)}} + {H_2}{O_{(l)}} + C{O_{2(g)}}\xrightarrow{{}}\mathop {Ca{{(HC{O_3})}_2}aq}\limits_{{\text{(soluble}}\,\;{\text{in water)}}} {/tex}
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text:
Lime water Test: On passing the evolved CO2 gas through lime water, we find that lime water turns milky.
{tex}\mathop {Ca{{\left( {OH} \right)}_{2(aq)}}}\limits_{Lime\,\,water} + C{O_{2(g)}} \to \ \mathop {CaC{O_{3(s)}} + {H_2}{O_{(l)}}}\limits_{White\,\,precipitate} {/tex}
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text:
• All acids have H + ions in common.
  • Acids produce H + ions in solution which are responsible for their acidic properties.
  • All bases have OH− (hydroxyl ions) in common.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

cards.text:
• Acids produce H + ions in presence of water.
  • Bases when dissolved in water gives OH− ions.
cards.topic_name: What do All Acids and All Bases Have In Common?

flash_cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
Strength of an Acid or Base
Strength of acids and bases depends on the no. of H+ ion and OH- ion produced respectively.
With the help of a universal indicator we can find the strength of an acid or base as it shows different colours at different concentrations of hydrogen ions in a solution.
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
A scale for measuring hydrogen ion conc. in a solution called pH scale has been developed.
pH = Potenz in German means power.
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text: When an acid or base solution is added to the universal indicator, it produces a new colour which is used to find the pH value of the acid or the base solution by matching the colour with the colours on pH colour chart.
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text: The normal pH scale ranges from 0 to 14 as given below:
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text: Living beings carry out their metabolic activities within an optimal pH range.
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text: A neutral solution has a pH of exactly 7, while an acidic solution has a pH less than 7 and a basic solution a pH more than 7.
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
Basic Salts: Strong base + weak acid
pH value is more than 7
e.g. CaCO3, CH3COONa
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
Acidic Salts: Strong Acid + weak base
pH value is less than 7
e.q. NH4Cl, NH4NO3
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
Neutral Salts: Strong Acid + Strong base
pH value is 7
e.g. NaCl, CaSO4
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
pH of a solution is the negative logarithm to the base 10 of the hydrogen ion concentration expressed in mole per litre.
pH = –log10 (H+)
pH scale : It is a scale showing the relative strength of acids and alkalis.
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
• A universal indicator produces green colour in a neutral solution, pH = 7.
  • The colour changes from blue to violet as pH increases from 7 to 14.
  • The colour changes from yellow to pink and then to red as pH decreases from 7
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
IMPORTANCE OF pH IN EVERYDAY LIFE

  • pH in our digestive system: Dilute HCl (Hydrochloric acid) helps in the digestion of food (proteins) in our stomach. Excess acid in the stomach causes acidity (indigestion).  
  • Tooth decay caused by acids: The bacteria present in our mouth converts the sugar into acids. When the pH of acid formed in the mouth falls below 5.5, tooth-decaying starts. The excess acid has to be removed by cleaning the teeth with a good quality toothpaste because these kinds of toothpaste are alkaline in nature.
  • Soil of pH and plant growth: Most the plants have healthy growth when the soil has a specific pH (close to 7) range which should be neither alkaline nor highly acidic.
  • Sensitivity of plants and animals to pH range: Our body works within the pH range of 7.0 to 7.8.When pH of rain water is less than 5.6 (called acid rain) and it flows into rivers, pH of river water lowers which makes survival of aquatic life difficult.
cards.topic_name: How Strong are Acid or Base Solutions?

cards.text:
Sodium chloride (NaCl)
Sodium chloride is called a common salt. It is derived from seawater.
Rock Salt is mined like coal, is brown coloured and crystalline in shape.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of NaCl:

  1. It is used in cooking food.
  2. It is used as a preservative in pickles.
  3. It is used in the manufacture of soaps.
  4. It is used to melt ice.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Water of Crystallization: fixed number of water molecules present in on formula unit of salt.

  1. CuSO4.5H2O
  2. CaSO4.2H2O
  3. CaSO4.{tex}\frac12{/tex}H2O
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Plaster of Paris
CaSO4.2H2O {tex}\xrightarrow{{373K}}{/tex} CaSO4{tex}\frac12{/tex}H2Oa
When Plaster of Paris is mixed with water it changes to gypsum.
{tex}\underset{POP}{CaSO_{4^.}\frac12H_2O\;+1\frac12H_2O}\rightarrow\underset{GYPSUM}{CaSO_4.2H_2O}{/tex}
Making toys, decorative material and smoothening surfaces, plaster for fractured bones.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text: Gypsum is calcium sulphate dihydrate. When gypsum is heated to a temperature of 100 °C, it loses {tex}\frac34{/tex} of its water of crystallisation and forms plaster of paris.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of Plaster of paris:

  • Doctors use POP for supporting fractured bones.
  • For making toys, material for decoration.
  • For making surfaces smooth.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Plaster of paris is calcium sulphate hemihydrates. Its chemical formula is: {tex}CaSO_4\cdot \frac12H_2O{/tex}.
It is prepared by heating gypsum to a temperature of 100°C in a kiln; it loses {tex}\frac34{/tex} of its water of crystallisation and forms plaster of paris.
{tex}\underset{\mathrm{Gypsum}}{{\mathrm{CaSO}}_4\cdot2{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}\xrightarrow[{(373\;\mathrm K)}]{\mathrm{Heat}\operatorname{to}100^\circ\mathrm C}\underset{\mathrm{Plaster}\;\mathrm{of}\;\mathrm{paris}}{{\mathrm{CaSO}}_4\cdot\frac12{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}+\underset{\mathrm{Water}}{1\frac12{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}{/tex}
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
The water molecules which form part of the structure of the crystal are called water of crystallization.
Example: CuSO4{tex}\cdot{/tex}5H2O.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Properties of washing Soda:

  • It is transparent crystalline solid.
  • It is soluble in water.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text: Washing Soda (Na2CO3{tex}\cdot{/tex}10H2O) : Recrystallization of sodium carbonate gives washing soda. It is a basic salt. Na2CO3 + 10H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na2CO3{tex}\cdot{/tex}10H2O.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Baking soda is a substance added in food for its faster cooking. Its chemical name is sodium hydrogen carbonate.
Sodium hydrogen carbonate is produced on large scale by reacting a cold and concentrated solution of sodium chloride with ammonia and carbon dioxide.
NaCl + NH3 + H2O + CO2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NaNCO3 + NH4Cl
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of bleaching powder:

  • For bleaching cotton and linen in the textile industry and for bleaching wood pulp in the paper industry.
  • Used for disinfecting drinking water to make it free of germs.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Bleaching powder is manufactured from chlorine gas.
It is produced by the action of chlorine on dry slaked lime [Ca(OH)2].
Ca(OH)2 + Cl2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CaOCl2 + H2O
It is represented as CaOCl2.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of  Sodium  hydroxide:
H2 : Fuels, margarine,ammonia for fertilisers
Cl2 : Water treatment,PVC
NaOH : Degreasing metals, soaps and paper making·
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text: Acid-base indicators are dyes or mixtures of dyes which are used to indicate the presence of acids and bases.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of Washing Soda:

  1. manufacture of Borax.
  2. glass, soap and paper industries
  3. cleansing agent for domestic purposes
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Preparation Washing Soda:
Recrystallisation of sodium carbonate.
Na2CO3 + 10H2O {tex}\xrightarrow{{Heat}}{/tex} Na2CO3.10H2O
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of Sodium Hydrogen Carbonate:

  1. Used as ingredients of antacids
  2. Used in soda-acid extinguishers.
  3. or preparing baking soda (baking powder + mild edible acid)
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Preparation of Sodium Hydrogen Carbonate:
Used in baking/cooking
Heating NaHCO3 + H2O + CO2
NaHCO3 {tex}\xrightarrow{{heat}}{/tex} Na2CO3 + H2O + CO2
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Sodium Hydrogen Carbonate: NaHCO3
Common name - Baking Soda. It is mild corrosive base
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of Sodium Hydroxide:

  1. Used as disinfectant of water
  2. Used as an oxidising agent.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Preparation of Sodium Hydroxide:
Prepared by the method called chlor-alkali process. It is called so because we get chlorine and an alkali (NaOH) in this process.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text: Sodium Hydroxide: NaOH, Common Name - caustic soda.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
• Salts of strong acid and a strong base are neutral, with a pH value of 7. For Example: NaCl, Na2SO4.
  • Salts of strong acid and weak base are acidic, with a pH value less than 7. For Example: Ammonium chloride solution has pH value of 6.
  • Salts of weak acid and strong base are basic, with a pH value more than 7. For Example: Sodium carbonate solution has a pH value of 9.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Common Salt:
​​​​​​​Chemical name: Sodium chloride
Common salt is a neutral salt and can be prepared in the laboratory by the reaction of sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid.
NaOH(aq) + HCl(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NaCl(aq) + H2O(aq)
It is an important raw material for products of daily use such as NaOH, baking soda, washing soda and bleaching powder.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Sodium hydroxide is produced by the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of sodium chloride (called brine).
The process is called the chlor-alkali process because of the products formed, i.e. ‘chlor’ for chlorine and ‘alkali’ for sodium hydroxide.
2NaCl(aq) + 2H2O(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NaOH(aq) + H2(g) + Cl2(g)
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of Baking soda:

  1. It is used as an antacid to remove acidity of stomach because it neutralises the excess acid present in the stomach and relieves indigestion
  2. It is used in fire extinguishers.
  3. Used to make baking powder, which is used in preparation of cakes, breads, etc.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts

cards.text:
Uses of  Washing Soda  :

  • In glass, soap and paper industry.
  • Manufacture of borax.
  • Cleaning agent for domestic purposes.
  • For removing permanent hardness of water.
cards.topic_name: More about Salts
